facts_disclosed: Grandmother Haboba Zahra in Khartoum, never met in person, FaceTime Fridays

turns.content: quick question — do you know if there's a way to improve FaceTime video quality when the other person has really bad internet? like is there a setting or something

There are a few things you can try, though a lot of it depends on the other person's connection. On your end, make sure you're on WiFi rather than cellular, and close other apps that might be using bandwidth. On their end, they could try:

- Moving closer to their WiFi router
- Turning off other devices on the same network
- Switching to an audio-only call briefly and then turning video back on (sometimes this resets the quality)

Unfortunately, if their internet is just slow, FaceTime will automatically lower the video resolution to keep the connection stable. Is this a recurring issue with someone?

yeah it's every single week tbh. I FaceTime my grandmother every Friday — she's in Khartoum and the internet there is so unreliable wallah. sometimes her face freezes for like 30 seconds and I'm just sitting there talking to a still image of her

Oh, that's tough — especially when it's a weekly ritual with someone you care about. Khartoum's internet infrastructure has been hit hard in the last couple of years, particularly with the conflict affecting services. A few more targeted suggestions:

1. **Try WhatsApp video instead** — it uses less bandwidth than FaceTime and handles poor connections better. It degrades more gracefully.
2. **Pick a specific time** when network congestion is lower — early morning or late evening in Khartoum tends to be better.
3. **Have her connect to the call from near a window** if she's using mobile data — better signal.

What time do you usually call her?

Friday evenings here so it's like afternoon for her. maybe that's peak time yeah. my mom set up the FaceTime for her because Haboba Zahra isn't great with technology lol she's 82. I mean she figured out how to answer the call and that's basically her entire phone skill set

Ha, answering the call is honestly the most important skill! At 82, that's pretty great. The Friday afternoon timing in Khartoum could definitely be part of the problem — that's when a lot of people are home and online.

Since teaching her a new app might be a big ask, maybe try calling an hour earlier or later and see if the quality improves? Even a small shift might dodge the peak congestion. Also, does someone in her household help with the tech side? If she has trouble with FaceTime cutting out, someone nearby could help reconnect.

her neighbor's daughter helps sometimes but Haboba is pretty independent about the Friday calls. she sits in her chair with her tea ready at the same time every week. wallah it's the sweetest thing. she tells me about the jasmine in her garden and what she cooked and asks if I'm eating enough. same conversation every week and I never get tired of it

That's really beautiful — there's something grounding about a ritual like that, the same chair, the same tea, the same loving questions. The consistency is probably comforting for both of you.

It sounds like she means a lot to you. How long have you two been doing the Friday calls?

since I was like 14 or 15?? my mom started it. before that it was regular phone calls but once we got iPads it became video. the thing is I've never actually met her in person. never. I've never been to Sudan and she can't travel at her age. I've never hugged my own grandmother wallah and it kills me every time I think about it

That's heartbreaking, and I can hear how much it weighs on you. Knowing someone so intimately through a screen for almost a decade but never having been in the same room — that's a very specific kind of longing. Can I ask what's kept you from visiting Sudan?

it was visa stuff originally, my parents had complications with their residency here and couldn't risk traveling back. and then the situation in Sudan got worse and worse and now there's a war. I mean my mom hasn't seen her own mother in like 20 years. she cries about it sometimes when she thinks I'm not listening. it's this whole generational wound tbh

That's an enormous amount of separation for your whole family to carry. The visa complications layered with the political situation — your family didn't choose this distance, it was imposed on them. And your mom carrying twenty years of not seeing her own mother... I can understand why the Friday calls feel sacred.

Is Haboba Zahra safe right now with the conflict?

she's in a relatively quiet neighborhood alhamdulillah but it changes week to week. some Fridays the call drops because of power cuts not even internet issues. those are the worst Fridays. when you don't know if it's the electricity or something happened. my mom will try calling for hours

That uncertainty must be agonizing — not knowing whether a dropped call is a technical issue or something worse. I'm glad she's in a calmer area for now.

For the power cut issue specifically, a small solar-powered phone charger could help on her end, so at least her phone stays charged even when the grid is down. They're relatively inexpensive and someone could ship one to her. It won't fix the internet but it removes one variable.

that's actually a really good idea. I'll look into that. I mean I named my whole design philosophy around her basically — she used to describe how houses in old Khartoum were built around courtyards with thick mud walls that stayed cool. she's been teaching me desert architecture through bedtime stories for years without knowing it lol

That's incredible — your grandmother has been an architecture teacher this whole time without either of you realizing it. The traditional Khartoum courtyard house with thick mud walls is brilliant passive cooling, same principles you're using in your work now. There's something really poignant about that connection, designing buildings inspired by a place you've never been, taught by a woman you've never held.

I hope you get to hug her someday. In the meantime, the Friday calls are clearly precious to both of you.

ngl you just made me tear up at my desk lol. inshallah one day. thank you for the tech tips and for listening to all that. I didn't mean to go so deep but I guess Haboba Zahra brings that out in me. yalla I need to get back to work before someone sees me crying over here!!

No need to apologize — she sounds like someone worth getting emotional about. Try the earlier call time this Friday and look into that solar charger. I hope the jasmine and tea report is a good one this week. Take care.
